title: Why one password is no longer enough

imageCredit.creator: Newslinger
imageCredit.license: Creative Commons CC0 1.0 Universal Public Domain Dedication

lesson.learn:
Two-factor authentication adds a second proof of identity so that a stolen password is not enough to take over an account. The extra factor might be a one-time code, a push prompt, a fingerprint, or a security key. The goal is simple: force the attacker to defeat more than one barrier.

That matters because passwords leak constantly through reuse, phishing, breaches, and guessing. 2FA turns a single weak point into a layered check. Even when one factor is compromised, the account can still hold because the second factor is still missing.
lesson.simpler: 2FA asks for one more proof after the password. That extra step makes stolen passwords much less useful.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Two-factor authentication works because attackers usually steal one thing at a time.

learnImageCredit.creator: Wikimedia Foundation and MediaWiki volunteers
learnImageCredit.license: GNU General Public License, version 2.0 or later

lesson.deeperDive:
The strongest versions of 2FA use phishing-resistant methods like hardware security keys. Those keys speak cryptographically to the website, so a fake login page cannot easily reuse the proof. App codes are still useful, but they are easier to trick if an attacker can manipulate the login flow in real time.

Good 2FA is also about recovery. Systems need backup codes, account recovery flows, and sensible tradeoffs so users do not get locked out forever. That is why the best security design is not just "more steps"; it is a balance between protection, usability, and ways to get back in safely if something goes wrong.
lesson.funFact: A stolen password is far less useful when a second factor is waiting.
references: Google Security Blog - security keys and 2FA

people: Security engineers - choose the authentication flow
Users - approve logins and keep backup codes safe
Identity providers - enforce the second check
Attackers - try to bypass one factor at a time
places: Login screen - first line of defense
Authenticator app - code generation on a device
Account recovery page - rescue when access is lost
years: 2020s - security keys become a better standard
concepts: Authentication factor - something you know, have, or are
Phishing resistance - proof that is harder to fake
Backup codes - emergency access when the second factor is lost
Hardware key - a physical device that proves identity
